claims.claim: BMWK-commissioned report states German data centres had an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, far higher than colocation-only Statista figures for Germany, implying large non-colo (enterprise/self-operated) capacity uncounted in colo-based denominators.
claims.sourceTitle: Status and development of the German data centre landscape – Executive Summary
claims.excerpt: With over 2,000 data centres and an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW, Ger many is already the largest centre for digital infra structure in Europe.

claims.claim: German Data Center Impact Report gives a much lower total IT power figure for Germany (colocation + enterprise data centers >50kW) than the BMWK total, showing definitional divergence in what counts as 'total installed load.'
claims.excerpt: 1,955 MW IT power in Germany approx.

claims.claim: Frankfurt colocation IT power reported at 831 MW, used as a sub-national benchmark for Germany's largest colo market.
claims.excerpt: 831 MW IT power in Frankfurt colocation data centers

claims.claim: Statista's 2025 colocation and scale-colocation IT power table lists Germany at 1,737 MW, well below BMWK's 2,700 MW+ total installed figure for the same country.
claims.sourceTitle: Europe: Colocation data center power by country 2031| Statista

claims.claim: Statista's 2025 colocation table lists Netherlands at 951 MW, matching the value used in geo.NL's derivation.
claims.sourceTitle: Europe: Colocation data center power by country 2031| Statista

claims.claim: Statista's 2025 colocation table lists Switzerland at 274 MW, matching the value used in geo.CH's derivation.
claims.sourceTitle: Europe: Colocation data center power by country 2031| Statista

claims.claim: Statista's 2024 colocation table shows Germany at 1,458 MW, Netherlands at 915 MW, and Switzerland at 243 MW, indicating year-over-year colo growth rates that differ by country.
claims.sourceTitle: Europe: Colocation data center power by country 2031| Statista

claims.claim: Bitkom's 2025 study reports a much higher total German data-center/IT-installation capacity figure than either BMWK or DCIRD, including cloud, traditional and edge segments.

Bitkom breaks down Germany's total IT connection power by facility size: large data centers (>5MW) account for 50%, medium (100kW-5MW) 23%, and small IT installations 27% — a size-based split independent of colocation vs. enterprise/on-prem categorization.
claims.excerpt: Große RZ > 5 MW Kleine IT-Installationen Mittelgroße RZ 100 kW - 5 MW in Prozent 50 23 27

claims.claim: Germany's data-center sector is subject to explicit efficiency requirements under the Energy Efficiency Act, a regulatory driver behind renewed public and market attention.
claims.excerpt: Data centers have recently become the focus of public attention due to the German Energy Efficiency Act, which places explicit efficiency requirements on existing and new facilities.

claims.claim: CBRE reports 5.8 GW of data centre capacity expected across Europe by end of 2024, a macro demand benchmark not broken out by country in this report.
claims.excerpt: 5.8GW Data centre capacity expected across Europe by the end of 2024

claims.claim: CBRE reports 85% of European colocation take-up last year was concentrated in the FLAPD markets (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, Dublin), excluding Zurich/Switzerland from the top-tier demand cluster.
claims.excerpt: 85% Of take-up was recorded in FLAPD* last year *Represents the five largest colocation markets in Europe: Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin

claims.claim: The Dutch Data Center Association's 2025 report frames the market narrative as moving 'From Boom to Bottlenecks,' signaling supply-side capacity constraints rather than providing an updated total-installed-load MW figure.
claims.sourceTitle: State of the Dutch Data Centers 2025 - Dutch Data Center Association
claims.excerpt: this year themed: “From Boom to Bottlenecks.” This special edition marks ten years of independent data center analysis

claims.claim: German data-center counts vary across sources: BMWK cites 'over 2,000' data centres while DCIRD cites 1,994 and Bitkom cites 'ca. 2.000... und 100 ab 5MW', reflecting inconsistent inventory definitions even before MW totals are considered.
claims.excerpt: 1,994 data centers* thereof 309 colocation data centers thereof 69 % in colocation data centers

tensions.text: BMWK's total German installed IT power (>2,700 MW, Jan 2025) is ~1,000 MW higher than Statista's colocation-only Germany figure (1,737 MW, 2025) used as the numerator basis for CE-7 colo apportionment. If ~35-40% of Germany's real installed load sits outside colocation, the colo-only denominator (CE-7 colo 3,451 MW) that geo.NL and geo.CH divide into is structurally biased toward whichever countries have lower non-colo shares — undermining the top-down capacity apportionment method used for both nodes.

Three German 'total capacity' figures disagree sharply — BMWK 2,700 MW+ (Jan 2025), DCIRD 1,955 MW (colo+enterprise>50kW), and Bitkom 2,980 MW (2025, cloud+traditional+edge) — a spread of over 1,000 MW depending on scope. This directly contradicts the search intent's premise that 'an existing/purchasable dataset... fixes the denominator scope today'; no such single consistent total-installed-load dataset was found, meaning geo.NL and geo.CH's colo-based denominator remains unvalidated against any agreed total-load standard.

For Switzerland, no new source in this search round narrows the gap already flagged in geo.CH's own provenance note between the colo-only Statista figure (274 MW) and the installed-base/self-operated-inclusive Mordor estimate (850.6 MW) or CBRE Suisse's commercial-only 340 MW; CBRE's 2025 Switzerland report instead provides only pan-European aggregates (5.8GW Europe-wide, 85% FLAPD take-up), leaving geo.CH's wide sensitivity band (0.07–0.13) empirically unresolved.

absences: No published total-installed-load (colocation + enterprise on-premise + self-operated) country-by-country MW breakdown for the CE-7 set (NL, DE, CH, plus others) using a single consistent methodology was found — the exact denominator-fixing dataset the queries sought does not appear to exist in retrieved sources.

No updated or more granular CBRE Suisse / Mordor figure disaggregating Swiss self-operated versus colocation capacity beyond what is already cited in geo.CH's derivation (340 MW commercial-only vs. 850.6 MW installed-base) was surfaced.

No numeric total-installed IT load figure (beyond colocation-only capacity) for the Netherlands was found in the 2025 DDA report text retrieved; the report's 'From Boom to Bottlenecks' theme is described qualitatively without a companion enterprise/self-operated MW figure.

No harmonized definitional standard (e.g., minimum kW threshold, inclusion/exclusion of self-operated or edge/traditional installations) was found reconciling BMWK's >100kW threshold, DCIRD's >50kW threshold, and Statista's colocation-only scope — the absence of standardization is itself an obstacle to fixing the denominator scope as intended.
